title: IEA Warns $6.5T at Risk From China Rare Earth Curbs, US LNG Set to Become Second-Largest Export Industry, and Hormuz Traffic Collapses to 13 Ships
subhead: The IEA's Global Critical Minerals Outlook puts a staggering number on China's leverage over downstream manufacturing, S&P Global projects LNG will transform the US trade balance within five years, and the Strait of Hormuz has gone from a trickle to nearly empty as US-Iran strikes enter a sixth consecutive day.

sections.heading: IEA: China Rare Earth Controls Threaten $6.5 Trillion in Global Production
sections.body:
The International Energy Agency released its Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2026 on July 16, warning that full implementation of China's rare earth export restrictions could place $6.5 trillion per year of downstream industrial production outside China at risk. The figure covers the automotive, aerospace, defense, electronics, and clean energy sectors. A separate $300 billion in annual downstream production would be at risk if China fully enforces proposed graphite export controls, given its over 90% share of global processed graphite output.

China expanded export controls on heavy rare earth elements in October 2025, adding new licensing requirements. While full implementation was postponed to November 2026, the IEA says the proposed measures reveal how concentrated supply chains remain. IEA Executive Director Fatih Birol said the report shows 'vast amounts of economic value depend on relatively small volumes of critical minerals, whose supply chains remain highly concentrated and are therefore vulnerable.'

There are signs of diversification. The IEA says China's share of global rare earth refining fell to 85% in 2025, down from 90% in 2023, and could decline to 70% by 2035 if all planned projects are completed. Public financing commitments for new critical mineral projects quadrupled to $65 billion between 2023 and 2025. New refining projects in the United States and Malaysia have begun to erode Beijing's dominance. But Birol cautioned that diversified supply 'can come at a higher cost' that functions as 'a mineral security premium in a time of geopolitical uncertainty.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first time the IEA has quantified the full downstream exposure from China's rare earth and graphite curbs in a single figure. The $6.5T number will shape policy debates in Washington, Brussels, and Tokyo about how fast alternative supply chains can scale — and whether the cost premium of diversification is insurance worth paying.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Collapses to 13 Ships as US-Iran Strikes Enter Sixth Day
sections.body:
Commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has ground to a near-standstill. Only 13 vessels passed through on Wednesday, the first full day of the reinstated US naval blockade, according to maritime intelligence firm Kpler. That compares to 23 ships the prior week and 47 the week before that, against a pre-conflict daily average of 138 ships. The seven-day moving average of crude oil flows through Hormuz fell to 5.5 million barrels per day through Wednesday, down from 9.4 million bpd the previous week, per Bloomberg calculations based on Kpler and Vortexa data.

The US military disabled the Curacao-flagged VLCC Belma near Kharg Island after the tanker ignored multiple warnings, firing a Hellfire missile into its smokestack. The ship was attempting to reach Iran's main oil export terminal. US Central Command said it conducted verification boardings of other vessels as part of the blockade enforcement.

Iran's military declared the Strait of Hormuz an 'unbreakable red line' and threatened to target 'all infrastructure throughout the region' if the US strikes Iranian civilian or energy facilities. Brig. Gen. Ebrahim Zolfaghari said Iran would 'never allow the United States to interfere' with the waterway. Iran also asked its Houthi allies to close the Red Sea oil route if the US strikes Iranian power infrastructure, according to Reuters.

Some tankers are transiting Hormuz with transponders switched off. A Suezmax carrying 1 million barrels of Saudi crude exited the strait on Tuesday with its AIS system disabled, Kpler data showed. Iran-linked vessels are still using the northern route approved by Tehran.
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz throughput has dropped over 90% from normal levels. The dual threat — US enforcement of a naval blockade and Iranian attacks on vessels using non-approved routes — has effectively shut the strait to normal commercial traffic. Iran's ask to the Houthis about the Red Sea would, if acted on, threaten the other key Middle East maritime chokepoint simultaneously.

sections.heading: US Expands Strikes Into Northern Iran, Hits Bandar Abbas Infrastructure
sections.body:
The US military carried out its sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iran early Friday, expanding the target set beyond the coastal and southern regions. For the first time in this escalation cycle, strikes reached areas around Tehran and Semnan province, home to Iran's ballistic missile production and space program. Iranian state media also reported strikes in Bushehr — site of Iran's only civilian nuclear power plant — as well as Ahvaz, Hamedan, Khuzestan, Lorestan, Markazi, and Sistan-Baluchestan provinces.

In Bandar Abbas, Iran's largest commercial port overlooking Hormuz, US strikes hit an airport, a railway station, and two bridges, killing at least seven people including conscripts and wounding over 300 since the renewed strikes began, per Iranian state media. An attack on Greater Tunb Island targeted Iranian defense and missile sites at the mouth of the strait.

Iran retaliated by launching missiles and drones at Bahrain, Jordan, and Kuwait, all hosting US military bases. Kuwait's military said it intercepted hostile missiles and drones, with explosions heard in the country. Qatar's Ministry of Defence reported repelling a missile attack, with a child injured by shrapnel from interceptions. Qatar earlier elevated its national security threat level to 'high.'
sections.whyItMatters: The geographic expansion of US strikes — from coastal and Hormuz-adjacent targets to Tehran's outskirts and Iran's missile production heartland — signals a deliberate escalation beyond just pressuring Iran over the strait. The strikes on Bandar Abbas infrastructure directly degrade Iran's commercial logistics capacity, while Iran's retaliation against Gulf states hosting US bases raises the risk of those states being drawn deeper into the conflict.

sections.heading: Senate Russia Sanctions Bill Gains 61 Co-Sponsors, Softened From 500% to 100% Tariffs
sections.body:
A US Senate bill imposing secondary sanctions on buyers of Russian oil and gas has gathered 61 co-sponsors, giving it a filibuster-proof majority. The legislation — a revised version of the Sanctioning Russia Act originally introduced in April 2025 — would impose tariffs of up to 100% on the top five buyers of Russian oil and natural gas, primarily targeting China and India. The bill also grants the US president authority to impose additional tariffs on countries that continue purchasing Russian energy.

The updated version is a softening from the original proposal, which called for 500% tariffs. The revised bill exempts 15 European nations from the secondary sanctions regime and gives the president waiver authority. India, which has become the largest buyer of Russian seaborne crude since the invasion of Ukraine, has pushed back diplomatically, arguing the tariffs would disproportionately affect developing economies.

The bill's momentum comes as the EU continues to purchase record volumes of Russian LNG, creating a tension between the sanctions push and actual European energy procurement. The exemption for 15 European countries appears designed to address this contradiction.
sections.whyItMatters: If passed, this would be the most aggressive US secondary sanctions regime on Russian energy to date, directly targeting India and China. The reduction from 500% to 100% tariffs suggests the sponsors are serious about passage — they've trimmed the most provocative elements. But the exemption for European buyers exposes the central problem: sanctioning Russia while still buying its gas.

sections.heading: US LNG Set to Become America's Second-Largest Net Export Industry
sections.body:
A new S&P Global Energy study projects that US LNG exports will contribute $1.4 trillion to GDP through 2040 and support 550,000 jobs annually, making liquefied natural gas the second-largest net export industry in the United States within five years, behind only civilian aircraft and parts.

Under current conditions, US feedgas demand for LNG exports is expected to double to 36 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) within five years, which is 25% higher than previous base case projections. The US is already the world's leading LNG supplier and is projected to surpass a one-third share of the global market. The study also found that US LNG has functioned as a domestic gas price shock absorber — during Winter Storm Fern, up to 9 bcfd of feedgas was redirected for domestic consumption.

However, the study warns that an extended pause in new export capacity investment — a scenario where no new LNG capacity is approved after 2025 — would reduce GDP by $1.3 trillion through 2040, raise domestic gas prices, and leave global gas markets more volatile. The findings come as the Iran-Hormuz conflict has demonstrated how dependent allied nations are on flexible US LNG supply.
sections.whyItMatters: The S&P study quantifies what the Hormuz crisis has already shown qualitatively: US LNG is becoming the cornerstone of allied energy security. The 25% upward revision to projected feedgas demand reflects both pre-war demand growth and the war-driven acceleration of LNG infrastructure buildout. If Washington restricts new export capacity, the economic hit would nearly equal the projected GDP contribution.

sections.heading: GCC States Accelerate Pipeline and Overland Bypass Investment
sections.body:
Gulf Cooperation Council states are moving beyond stopgap rerouting and investing in permanent infrastructure to bypass the Strait of Hormuz. According to Global Finance Magazine, much of GCC trade has been temporarily rerouted to Fujairah (UAE), Muscat (Oman), and Jeddah and Yanbu (Saudi Arabia), but governments are now studying pipeline projects, rail lines, and new roads running north through Iraq, Jordan, Syria, and Turkey.

The economic damage from the Hormuz disruption varies across the Gulf. The IMF projects Qatar's economy to contract by 8.6% this year, while Kuwait and Bahrain face contractions of 0.6% and 0.5%, respectively. Saudi Arabia, Oman, and the UAE are proving more resilient with projected growth of 3.1–3.6%, partly because they have existing bypass infrastructure like the East-West pipeline and Red Sea ports.

The conflict has also hit adjacent industries hard. GCC states produce 25–30% of global exports of ammonia, urea, phosphate, and sulfur, key fertilizer inputs. Regional aluminum output has fallen 40% from pre-war levels, with smelters in the UAE, Qatar, and Bahrain sustaining physical damage. The Gulf accounts for only 8% of global aluminum output but supplies 18% of European imports and 21% of US imports.
sections.whyItMatters: The 8.6% contraction projected for Qatar — the world's top LNG exporter — shows how hard the Hormuz shutdown hits economies with no alternative export route. The rush to build overland corridors through Iraq and Syria is a bet that Hormuz vulnerability is structural, not temporary. If these projects proceed, they would redraw Gulf energy logistics permanently.

bottomLine: Three stories dominate today's energy geopolitics. First, the IEA has put a number — $6.5 trillion — on China's rare earth leverage, a figure that reframes critical minerals from a supply chain concern into a macroeconomic vulnerability of the first order. Second, Hormuz is effectively closed: 13 ships on Wednesday versus 138 on a normal day, and Iran is now asking the Houthis to close the Red Sea too — a two-chokepoint scenario that would be catastrophic for global oil flows. Third, US LNG is poised to become America's second-largest export industry, a projection that has been accelerated, not slowed, by the Iran war. The Senate's Russia sanctions bill with 61 co-sponsors adds another layer: if passed, it would force a reckoning between Washington's sanctions posture and the reality that its allies and trading partners are still buying Russian energy in record volumes.
sources: IEA Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2026, S&P Global Energy US LNG Impact Study Phase 4, Bloomberg, Kpler, Vortexa, Reuters, France24, Gulf News, Rigzone, Oilprice.com, Global Finance Magazine, The Independent, KSL News, CNBC, Firstpost, Yahoo Finance, Independent, Congressional Research Service, IMF April projections, White House press briefing
